Okay, now we're going to step eight, salesmanship mentality. This is really good. This is kind of the underlying juice and mojo behind everything else you're going to do. If you have an ethical, good product or service that can genuinely help your prospect, then shame on you if you don't use every tactic and strategy available to persuade him to give it a try. Most people sell from their heels. What I mean by that is they kind of position themselves like this and they say, well, I've got this thing that, nah, I don't think you want it and they'll both. They're ready to give in right away. As we talked about, I think at the last section, we have an uneasy relationship with selling in this culture. We just generally say we don't like it, we don't want it, and yet that's how we buy. We buy or because we were made aware of it through advertising. Your job is to, you're doing good work. If you have something that's going to help somebody and it's a fair value and you're just making them aware that it's out there and then you're helping them to make the right decision because they need it. Selling from your heels, you're doing a disservice to the perfect prospect out there who can use, whose life can be better with what you offer. There are no natural salesmen. I know you've probably heard the term, yeah, he's a natural salesman. Salesmen are created through experience, insight, empathy, and formulas. That's why this simple writing system formula is so important. But experience makes it better. Insight is what I'm giving you here and empathy is what I want you to develop. Thinking about what the world looks like from your prospect's point of view. What is his day like? How does he feel about this? Not how you feel about it. How does he or she feel about this? As you gain more experience with this, as you start saying, well, what is it like to be in the position of the perfect prospect for what I offer? What is their day like? What are their emotions like? What keeps them up late at night? What actually would help them? What can I say or do or offer that will help them understand that once they have what I offer, things will be better? So, everyone sells all the time. If you're married, you sold yourself on your spouse. If you have a job, you sold yourself on the employer to hire you. If you have ever sold anything, you've sold yourself and you've sold whatever it was that you had to offer. So people don't like selling, but just get over it, grow up, basically. You're in the business of being in business, and business is all about selling. Everybody sells all the time. We just have this weird blind spot about the value of selling. So one of the things that I've encountered a lot in recent years has been, excuse me, people saying they will teach you how to, quote, sell without selling. There is no such thing. Experienced veteran marketers, writers, and businessmen understand that to sell is to sell. You don't need to be in a loud checkered jacket shouting at people. Whatever your caricature of a good salesman is, just get rid of it now. The best salesman is who you can be when you're at your most persuasive. When you're saying this is what can help you, here's what I have, it's a fair value, here's how what you should do next to be able to get it. When you're doing that, you are doing the good work of helping someone who needs what you have or can use what you have, and you're putting them in a position where they're okay with it and they understand it. So just get over the idea that you can sell without selling. The idea that you can somehow trick somebody or have some mild message that is going to trigger the response where they will say, well, okay, I'm going to buy because you know what, you're just doing a good job of making me feel okay about this. That doesn't really do it. It's really easy to get people up on the fence, as we say, to get them to say, yeah, it's a pretty good product and maybe someday down the road I will buy that. That's easy to do. What's hard to do is to knock them off the fence, is to get them to say, you know what, I need that right now and I'm going to buy it. Thanks for making this offer for me. Let's get down to business. What do I have to do now to get this? That's harder. That's where you have to use the tactics that I'm showing you. So get straight on your goal. This is your future if you're in business. You are trading product or services for money. It's a fair exchange. If you're providing honest value, then you have the right to be able to say what you need to say to persuade your perfect process to become a perfect customer. So one of the things I've been telling writers out there who are in the wrong mindset when they sit down to write is there is money out there that really belongs to you. I mean, that's a cute way of saying there are prospects out there who really need to understand how important it is to buy what you're offering. And the universe is off kilter until you correct this appalling situation. I have more professional writers, high level A-list writers come back to me and say, that's what really did it for them. When they heard me say, there's money out there that belongs to you and you've got to correct that situation. What that means is that there are people out there who can legitimately benefit from what you're offering. So do what you need to do to make that happen and get in the mindset that this is what you have to do. Without selling, you will fail at business, at life, at everything. Just get into this mindset where salesmanship is really a good thing. It's persuasion. It's being able to come from a position where you have credibility, where you can help people to make the decision that will really benefit them. This is why it's really important to have good ethical products to provide honest value. That's who I like to work with. In business, you will encounter a lot of people who are sociopaths who do not have your best interest in mind. Don't let that sour you on the greater good that being in business does, which is offering good products and good services at fair value. You set them up to knock them down and win them with the value that I've been talking about. Here's an example of an email from quite a while ago. Let me just read it and you'll see how I've incorporated this. The subject line is, Bob, seriously, it's time to get hip or give. Get worried. This was going to people in business. Hi, Bob. The really good part about marketing online is that the nature of doing biz on the web keeps changing. The playing field is constantly being leveled, which means that even rookie marketers and small biz owners with modest resources can take over an entire niche sometimes overnight if they're hip to the stunning new tactics that get leaked by the richest and best business honchos. The really bad part about this is that even if you're sitting pretty right now in your market, you can be toast tomorrow if you aren't paying attention. There's more to this. You can read it. This sets up. It's the good, bad type of thing. This is starting the sales process. This is setting up the story that's going to get you into a position where, well, what do you have? Okay, I agree with you. I'm in a desperate or I'm in a vulnerable situation. What you want to do is tickle his greed gland mercilessly. One of the words that people have trouble with is greed. I'm not going all Gordon Gekko on you, which was from the movie Wall Street where he said, greed is good. I don't think blind greed is necessarily good, but greed is not a bad word in and of itself. Greed is I want a better life for myself and my family. I want my business to thrive. I want my life to be the best it can be. That is what I'm talking about when I talk about his greed gland. When you understand how close we are to the jungle, a lot of people think that our ancestors were in the jungle and then were in this new shining civilization where everything has changed. Good salesmen understand that really we're kind of straddling it. We got one foot in the jungle and one foot in the modern civilization. In the jungle, status, fear, greed, and bragging rights still reign. When you're persuading people to do things, offering them status, addressing their fears, and certainly highlighting their fears and dealing with that. We know from even reading the daily papers that greed, wanting for myself the best thing that I can offer my family and my business, myself, and bragging rights are very, very important. It's the old story of the two people driving in with identical new cars and they get out and they start comparing who got the better deal and whoever got the better deal, however they want to measure it, has bragging rights for that. It's very important and if you aren't able to convince your prospect that he's going to be able to brag about the deal he got, then you may be in trouble. You use your relationship, your reputation, and your credibility with prospects whenever possible. This is why we're building trust in the first place. It's very hard to do what we call cold selling, which is go to someone who doesn't know you from Adam, who has never dealt with you before, doesn't know who you are, doesn't trust you at all. You can hold up from scratch this whole relationship. That's harder to do than going to your house list where you have a relationship, where your reputation precedes you, where you have some credibility. The best list you can go to are to list the people who have already bought from you or who have heard from you and signed up or said, yeah, okay, I want to hear more. Anybody who's taken the step from not knowing you at all, from being a stranger to being in some kind of a relationship, that makes it a lot easier to persuade. This is why building trust and establishing your credibility is so important. Here's an example. This is another email from a while back. Bob, I'm recommending this strongly. This goes to my house, this went to my house list, people who knew who I was. Hi, Bob. Anyone who's been receiving emails from me knows one thing. I do not recommend a lot of other courses and products. I'm picky because I know my reputation is on the line every time I stick my neck out and you don't risk a 25-year reputation on lousy stuff. I do, however, occasionally, parenthesis very occasionally, come right out and strongly urge people I know to check something out. I'd elbow you in the ribs if I was sitting next to you too, just to emphasize how critical it is that you move fast. This is one of those rare occasions. Then I go on with the pitch. That's taking advantage of the relationship that we have. I honestly feel this is a good deal, so I want to share this with you. In your bag of tricks that you have when you get to your salesmanship mentality, you have to think about what do you bring to the table that is unique or valuable? Keep that at the forefront of your mind. The table means it's the virtual table where everyone's sitting there and you're saying, I have this for sale or my services are available. What makes you different from the next guy? Why should I deal with you rather than someone else? What are you doing that is unique or valuable? This gets back to you, USP, of course. How does this position you to help others get a better deal or to do something more efficiently or get better results? I was thinking about positioning yourself, but when you position yourself as unique and valuable, this puts you in a situation where you can say, look, I'm in a position to give you a better deal. I have inside information that the competition can't have or won't share with you. I can give you better results based on my experience, based on my track record, things like this. Keep thinking in terms about what it is that makes you unique. One of the big things is what is the dramatic story of how you discovered a skill, a secret, information, or a particular product that you are now sharing? You aren't special. You just found a better way of doing things. I often tell people that there was nothing unique about me that led to my very long and very successful career. Other people can put their nose down to the grindstone like I did. Other people can work and go through things. That's why I like to help other writers and other marketers do what I've done. I've made all the mistakes. I like to say I've learned everything I know about marketing and writing by making a mistake, blundering into a situation, and then critically thinking about what went wrong, fixing it, filling my skillset, if there were holes in my skillset, and making it better so that the next time I went into that situation I would know what to do, I would do it better, I would handle it better, I could get better. I just did that throughout my career. For me teaching other people, I like to think I can be able to shortcut things because I made all the mistakes that I can now tell you the lessons that I learned so that you don't have to make the same blunders. There's nothing special about me. In fact, I made most of the mistakes possible during my early career. My only regret, however, is that I didn't make all the mistakes possible because that's where all the good lessons came from. Let me share the lessons that I have with you which hopefully can shortcut your journey so you don't have to go through all the bad stuff I did. Is your story unique because you worked hard, stumbled on something, or was taught a better way? How does it make you just like your prospect? Or how does the story make you different than your prospect? Sometimes you can say, look, I discovered something that's going to help you. The reason I got there was because I had advantages you don't have. I had opportunities that you'll probably never get, but that doesn't matter because what I have and what I'm sharing with you is going to make your life better. Because you would not normally be able to find out about this stuff, I'm going to let you know about it and on and on. Sometimes you want to be just like the prospect and I'm just like you. I've been broke most of my life. I've only been able to buy used cars and then I found a way that I can actually buy a new car for the price of a used car, for example. That would be, I'm just like you, but I've stumbled on a way where something can help you when I share it. Then sometimes it's like, I've always had new cars because I've worked in an industry where I was able to get better deals than the average civilian on this. However, I found a way where I think you can actually take advantage of the same situations that I had, even though I had privileges that you won't be able to have. You're different, but you're sharing the information. Think about terms of that. One of the best things in old time salesmen taught me early in my career was the concept of the grease slide. The grease slide is about, imagine your sales message as literally a slide with grease on it so that you want to put your prospect on at the top of the slide, giving just an ever-so-gentle push, but because it's greased, he's going to go faster and faster spinning down around until he reaches the end. You want it to be a breathless ride. That means you don't want tangents. You don't want to say, okay, stop. Hold on to yourself. Settle down. Have a cup of coffee. I'm going to tell you a long story about where I came from and why this is important. Don't do that. Make it a breathless ride. Make sure that everything flows smoothly, and you don't want to give him any time to stop and go do something else. You want to have that riveting, yet casual, face-to-face, bar room conversation that we've talked about that gooses him out of his inherently hostile, synambulant blob state and put him on a ride that gives him no time to catch a breath until you arrive at the close. Now, we'll talk more about that bar room conversation in just a bit, but it's the idea that there is no, once they're on the ride, there is no stopping. There is no time out. It goes as fast as possible. With long copy, believe it or not, people will read some of the ads I've shown you, the whole ads were two and three pages long in magazines. People didn't do anything else until they finished reading. They didn't stop. They didn't take a phone call. They actually put everything else on hold and went deep into this, and I took them into another world, and that's what you want to strive for, the grease slide. Here's an example. Here's the insider secret. The expensive big ad agencies don't want you to discover. How to create your first damn good ad in just nine minutes. You don't even have to actually write it. This is a tested and proven shortcut developed by one of the highest paid and most sought after veteran copywriters in the biz that is working right now for other people to bring in proven profits. It's fast. It's simple. It's easy, and you're about to discover how to do it yourself for free, then from John Carleton, and we go on to that. That's an example of starting the grease slide and pushing them off on it. I also talked about nine minutes. How do you time this if you want? Nine minutes is all you need, and then we go off on to that. All those tactics are related to the idea of making this a breathless ride that's going to go very quick. Here's an example of salesmanship mentality. This is a piece that ran on a website. It's also to a golf audience. The superscript above the headline says, I've set aside a $49 video in your name, and it's absolutely free. How to guarantee you're never the guy playing but ugly golf this summer from Dr. Michael Larry, president of OHP Golf. He says, right now in the dead of winter, there are only two kinds of golfers. One, those who live where it's warm and you can still squeeze in a game or two, even in February. Two, and the rest of the golfing world, which spends the winter sadly watching snow and rain fall on their favorite course, all locked up for the season. What's more important are the two kinds of golfers that exist in the spring, and then we get off into it. This is a way of using the relationship that he has to get into this with an interesting story and talk about but ugly golf, which is slang for, it's a term that the fancy magazines would never use in a headline or in an article because they think it's undignified, but because we know that our audience actually appreciates a little undignified humor once in a while and knows what but ugly golf looks like because a lot of them have engaged in it, that this kind of headline starts the conversation they've been waiting for for a long time and they love that people level with them and are honest. The classic F then setup is very, very good. Let me see if I have the example here. Yes. Here's an example of what we call the F then. So F, you've ever dreamed of becoming the kind of bass player who scares other musicians with your skills and ability to drive the beat, dot, dot, dot. This will be the most exciting message you ever read. So originally I had then this will be the most exciting thing. So it's F, you're in this situation, then this is going to be the most exciting message you ever read. This is going to be a wonderful opportunity for you. This is going to be something you're really glad you stumbled on, whatever. So that F then thing is a great way to start any kind of sales message if you're stuck for other ways to start it because you've kind of set up what's happening and you want them to get into this. So that salesmanship mentality, it's very, very important. It's something that most people don't get to learn about unless they get to hang out with a lot of old time salesmen. And I cut my teeth working with guys who had been salesmen in the street going door to door selling encyclopedias who understood the value of being able to persuade someone face to face and then took those skills and use them when they were using direct response advertising. That's why I say your ad or your sales message, your email, your video, whatever is like a little salesman that you send out into the world and does the job that you would do face to face if you had the opportunity to sit down face to face with every perfect prospect you have. So really, really understand what it's like to think like a salesman and every part of your life will start to get better. All right, salesmanship question and answer. The questions get very esoteric in people looking into the grade of bis at this point sometimes. Rather than go down that path because you might have questions like that. And obviously, if you're in the coaching program or you want to float something larger to your coach to get some feedback by all means, but I'd rather focus on some of the stories that you've told me over the years, I think would also help people wrap their head around because this is all about mindset. This is one of the few modules that's not really a very concrete do this thing. This is getting yourself clear to do the next steps. You often say like, you know, who would we rather work with? So if there's we have the choice between this person as a PhD in English or some language and this person's been didn't graduate high schools but been selling cookbooks door to door. Who's who's probably going to be more successful at the end of the simple. Yeah, I've often said that while I enjoy, I've enjoyed writing myself. I've actually, I actually was a pretty good writer most of my life. However, what was missing for me was salesmanship. And that's why it became so important for me when I taught other writers how to become successful as copywriters at the salesmanship was the main thing. And I always said I would rather take a illiterate but street wise salesman and teach him how to write copy than to try to take someone with a PhD in English literature and try to teach him how to sell because there's more resistance from the person who thinks I know how to write. I don't you know, the salesmanship is easy. Well salesmanship is actually a little more difficult than the actual act of writing. The you know, a lot of my friends are a list writers. I hang out. I'm lucky enough to hang out with a large group of the best writers in the world actually and we hang out and we we have a lot of fun together. And we privately talk about who is the better writer who has a better command of the language who has who has the best writing skills, which is often not the same thing at all with who sells the most who gets the most done in the world as a marketing juggernaut. So so if push comes to shove you want a salesman you can always figure in fact I think I had the ad about the lazy businessman's three step shortcut to to a great sales the first your first great sales piece, which is that if you have a salesman who knows how to sell you can record him and then transcribe it and then fix it a little bit and that's often your great first ad because the face to face conversation that salesmen have where they encounter objections they they they they are able to solve things as you go in real time. If you can capture that that's like magic in a bottle. And that's what that's that's what we're really trying to get to is get that one on one conversation into your video or your ad or your or your audio or whatever whatever vehicle you're using and send that little salesman out into the world to reach multiple people so you don't have to do it face to face. Exactly. Let's talk a little bit about the reality check about whether you're new to this world of marketing or you're you're ready to step up your game which is why you're in the simple writing system. A statement I've made that so far no one has held me as give me a concrete here's an example is in the history of American cinema as far as I know there has never been a movie made that is widespread popularity where the salesman or the salesperson has been the hero with the only exception of they've redeemed themselves. They start off the movie as salespeople which is thus by being not salespeople somehow that isn't itself heroic. So let's just give some feedback on like what is what is the reality of how you're going to have to in your own head get clear with this that selling does is not inherently evil despite the fact you may have been told this your entire life by well-meaning parents. Yeah I think you said you said at first we have a cultural bias against salesman. They tend to be the bad guy in stories when in truth salesmen just get a bad rap and advertising gets a bad rap all over it's a it's a well-deserved black eye in many cases going back to the to the snake oil salesman. There's been a lot of bad advertising for unethical bad products out there and that gives everyone in the business a black eye. That's why I always say I only want to deal with ethical people who have a good product who provide honest value then it's easy but you still have to use the same tactics the tactics aren't to blame. What's to blame is the same stuff that persuades people to do something not in their best interest can be used to do something that is in their best interest. That's why it's almost incumbent upon ethical good marketers to learn these tactics so you can battle against the unethical marketers out there who are just taking advantage of people knowing that persuasion the art of persuasion you know matters with you know the essential things like trust and credibility which people have lied about or people have concocted stories you know the we hear about these constant stream of financial scams and things that go on that's where the word con man comes from it's a confidence scheme it's the word con means confidence you build confidence in the people and then you screw them essentially well okay those people should be pillory they should be put away for a long time but for the average businessman for the guy who's got a donut shop or the or the businessman who provides services you know such as accounting or to the average lawyer or chiropractor or dentist or to the person who's selling information on how to make your business better and how to write advertising you're offering good stuff the tactics used establish your credibility make sure you're trustworthy give them an honest reason to to look further into this and give them reasons why this is not such a scary thing and why they're not risking a lot and we'll talk about risk reversal in a bit and why this is a good deal and why they should do it now so just get get straight in your head that you're you're a salesman now you have a business you is your job to be able to convince your perfect prospect that yes you are the you are the person they should be dealing with yours is the product they should be they should be buying yours is the service they should be using it doesn't happen because you're just a wonderful person as i i like to say the marketing graveyard is crammed to bursting with superior products that failed and died in ugly death because nobody figured out how to sell them nobody figured out how to market them the the the stuff we use today in a lot of different uh uh marketing areas are not necessarily the best products they just happen to be sold better and sometimes the best product and the best man doesn't win the game yeah exactly you touched just to kind of wrap this up different people now in terms of motivation what motivates you but you had a couple i'm just going to restate you you may be personally motivated because you sincerely believe you have a product or service that will help people but you you initially have what are substantially if not a hundred percent altruistic reasons for wanting to get this out in the world the reality is if you cannot get people to exchange some amount of money for this thing unless you have your life does not have money problems unless you are otherwise have no need to raise money for any reason that there's no issue in your life that there's plenty might solve all those things you can't help those people unless you can build a case that the only way you can help the next person in that queue is if the first person gave you enough money to support yourself to support your family so even if altruism is your motivation you've got to get over yourself and learn to sell i i have a lot of artist friends painters sculptors people who work in ceramics superior stuff great stuff but whenever we have the conversation about well let me help you with marketing and stuff they back off no way you know i want my stuff to sell because it's great and people should understand what good art is and that's why they should buy it and it doesn't work that way and that's why they spend their lives being miserable often poor and angry at the at the artists who do market their stuff who may be inferior but because they take the simple steps to market their stuff they are the ones who get all the media attention they charge high prices for their stuff and people buy it and i i keep telling these artists look your stuff is inherently good but nobody knows about it it's in your front room and nobody knows it even exists so take some simple steps to bring it out to make people aware of it to make people understand you know to to make people understand that yes there is better art available and it may be harder to find but here's how you can actually do it and they just say no they they don't want to do this because they are dead sent against selling stuff it's it doesn't change their art even in the slightest bit their art is the same stuff it was whether it's sold and it hangs in a house there were somebody paid a lot of money for it or whether it sits in their garage gathering dust for for the rest of their lives it's still the same artwork so why not do the things that need to be done to bring this out to the world find the perfect prospects and give them what they want the perfect prospect wants your art he just doesn't know it exists right another it's a traveling exercise i'll do with people who are dismissive of this write down five famous artists that you admire to take everyone take the piece of paper i assure you if you get even the smallest biography on these people's lives and there's only one name that appears on this list frequently this isn't true of that that person was an equally good salesperson as they were artists if you know anything about their life or with van there you go there yeah that's that's the the outlier is yes the one person that appears frequently died penniless young but the only reason we know of him is because he happened to have a brother who was one of the great salespeople of his time feel so yeah so this is true of whatever your passion is now is the time whether your passion is as you said like there's a dollar the universe is slightly out of kilter because i have something of value that people would gladly willingly part with money in exchange for you have to make that happen they will not come finding you and you have to as i said convince them now is the time to do it not someday so it's and again the exercise i'll we'll get to that so i think that's good we'll wrap this up now and you can give people the specifics on the exercise and then we'll keep moving through the system okay hope you had fun listening to this section it's one of my favorites all right here's your exercise for this particular section on salesmanship mentality so i want you to think about the bar room conversation that you have again you are sitting there innocently at a bar minding your own business and someone comes up and says to the bartender not to you pour me a double i've just had the worst day possible and the bartender says what's the matter bob and bob says blank whatever bob says identifies him as the perfect prospect for what you have if you're an accountant for example and i'm apologize for picking on accountants but it's so easy if you're an accountant he says my bookkeeping is a mess i'm going to be in trouble with the irs again this year and i'm not going to make my payments and all this and so he becomes a perfect prospect if you're selling information on grooming dogs uh you know at home and he comes in he says yeah the bob my my dogs are at the groomer still for yet another time their their hair is all locked up and things are horrible i wish i could do this by myself and save some money and so you when you turn to this perfect prospect what do you say and i want you to start thinking about what it is you would actually say to that person to begin the conversation where they would turn to you and not say why are you talking to me or they would say hey i'm not in the mood to talk to anybody or i don't know you so what what could you say where they would say really that's interesting and move on to the next point this begins the salesmanship process so think about why would someone deal with you and not your competitors or not with the competing things to be out there why would they in the dog grooming thing why would they want to work at home on their own dogs rather than drop them off at a groomer and you know the reasons maybe you're going to save money it's not as messy as people think you've got a really quick way to do it so the dogs can stay constantly groomed rather than waiting every two months to go in things things like that also talk about how your story how was your story unique and what is different about what you present as opposed to all the other options out there how are you like your prospect and how are you different than your prospect so you can actually begin the conversation hey i was in the same position you were in last month however i stumbled on an easy way that allowed me to do x and this addresses things so very quickly you get into this i was just i was in your situation i was exactly like you and here's what happened that kind of before and after thing or how are you different than your prospect you may say you know i've been lucky enough to have all these opportunities that i think most people don't have but i'm willing you know i'd like to share with you what i've learned and be even though you haven't had the opportunities i've had or you haven't had the privileges that i've had or you haven't had access to the networks that i've had you can now take advantage of this because i i can share it with you and uh finally how will his life be different with with what you offer how is what you have for him something that is revolutionary or something that is subtle but a major change but fixes or solves or affects the problem that he's having the reason he's he came into the bar and sat down he's frustrated how does what you have address that particular thing this is a way to get started very easily to kind of stick your foot into the water of salesmanship and i want you to think hard about this and write this stuff down just write down the first part of what you would say in that real face-to-face situation it's a nice easy way to smoothly glide into salesmanship mentality
